text: On inspection, there was no effusion or nodules concerning for metastatic disease.
The superior segmental nodule was not invading the upper lobe.
We incise the inferior pulmonary ligament to mobilize the lobe and dissect towards the inferior pulmonary vein and the posterior hilum.
Interlobar, AP, and subaortic lymph nodes were sampled and sent for frozen section.
The posterior hilum was explored and the superior segmental vein was identified.
The fissure was explored and superior segmental artery identified and encircled.
The pulmonary artery was divided using a powered thoracoscopic stapling device.
The pulmonary vein was again identified and encircled.
Pulmonary vein was subsequently divided using a powered thoracoscopic stapling device.
The superior segmental bronchus was identified.
Prior to dividing the bronchus, a compression test was performed to confirm isolation of the superior segmental bronchus.
The basilar segment inflated adequately whereas the superior segment did not inflate.
The superior segmental bronchus was then divided.
After dividing the segmental artery, vein and bronchus, the patient was given intravenous endocyanin green.
The thoracoscopic camera was set to near-infrared fluorescence imaging.
The ICG causes the perfused lung parenchyma to exhibit fluorescent green appearance, whereas the target segment exhibited no fluorescence.
The boundary between the fluorescent and non-fluorescent lung represents the intersegmental plane to demarcate the superior segment of the left lower lobe and is marked with electrocautery.
The superior segment of the left lower lobe was then resected with powered stapler.
Specimen was placed in a surgical glove.
The specimen was sent off for frozen section to confirm size criteria of under 2 centimeters.
Pathologist confirmed 1.3 centimeter superior segmental adenocarcinoma with clear margins and all nodes were negative for malignancy.
Chest tube was inserted and lung was reinflated under direct vision.
We then proceeded with closure.
